policy_statement: Establish national health alerts for dust storms and heat waves.
mechanism_description: Implementing national alerts for dust storms and heat waves enables earlier protective actions and preparedness, reducing exposure and associated health harms.
typology_evidence_quote: "Establish national health alerts for dust storms and heat waves."
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heat waves, dust storms/poor air quality) → early warning & risk communication → protective behaviors + service readiness → ↓ exposure/vulnerability → ↓ health harms
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human/system change: Government and public-health agencies implement and operate alert/early warning systems (thresholds, forecasting, messaging) that trigger individual guidance and institutional response plans (e.g., cooling center activation, outreach to vulnerable groups, work/activity modifications).  
(2) Mediating process: Timely information and coordinated response reduce contact with hazardous conditions (heat stress, particulate inhalation) and improve clinical/public-health readiness during peaks.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced morbidity and mortality during climate-amplified extremes, increasing population resilience/adaptive capacity to heat and air-quality hazards.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced health impacts by lowering exposure and improving preparedness for extreme heat and dust/air-quality events.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Hazard forecasting/alerts lead to behavior and service changes that reduce exposure and health impacts during extreme events.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: The mechanism’s main effect is risk reduction from climate hazards (heat, dust/PM), not emissions reduction (Mitigation), not primarily reduced resource use (Resource Efficiency), and not ecosystem-mediated benefits (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: Alerts can marginally affect energy use (e.g., increased cooling during heat alerts) or emissions through emergency operations, but these are secondary and not the dominant intended pathway.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy implementing health alerts for heat/dust/air-quality episodes fundamentally operates through preparedness and exposure reduction, so Adaptation remains primary across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype note: Any finer classification (e.g., “public health adaptation,” “early warning systems,” “heat-health action plans”) depends on policy-specific design details and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Dust storms can be driven by land use as well as climate; even when not purely climate-caused, the policy still functions as climate risk management for heat/air-quality extremes. Potential rebound (higher AC use during alerts) is a secondary consideration and does not change the primary classification.
co_benefits: Improved general emergency risk communication capacity; reduced non-climate air-pollution health impacts.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning-strategy if the underlying policy text specified formal trigger-based action plans and interagency protocols; however, the provided wording emphasizes alerts/communication rather than broader planning or mandated actions.
